topic: Land Revenue Systems — Permanent Settlement, Ryotwari & Mahalwari

questions.text: The Permanent Settlement of 1793 in Bengal was introduced under the tenure of which Governor-General?
options: Lord Wellesley
Lord Cornwallis
Lord Dalhousie
Warren Hastings
questions.explanation: The Permanent Settlement of 1793 was introduced by Lord Cornwallis (Governor-General 1786-1793). Under this system, the land revenue demand in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa was permanently fixed — zamindars would pay a fixed annual sum to the Company forever. In return, they were recognized as hereditary owners of the land. The 'Sunrise Rule' required payment before sunrise on the due date; failure meant the estate was auctioned. The system created a stable zamindar class but permanently disadvantaged cultivators who had no legal protection.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Permanent Settlement and Lord Cornwallis is one of the most frequently tested Governor-General/Viceroy-to-policy pairings in Modern History

questions.text: The Ryotwari System, under which revenue was directly settled with the cultivator, was associated with which region and administrator?
options: Bengal — Lord Cornwallis
North-Western Provinces — Holt Mackenzie
Madras — Thomas Munro
Punjab — R. Merttins Bird
questions.explanation: The Ryotwari System was introduced in Madras Presidency by Sir Thomas Munro (who later became Governor of Madras). Under this system, the British settled directly with each cultivating ryot (farmer), bypassing zamindars entirely. Revenue was assessed on each plot based on quality and periodically revised. Munro believed the Permanent Settlement's zamindar class was an artificial creation not suited to Madras's social structure. The system was later extended to Bombay by Mountstuart Elphinstone and also applied in Assam and Coorg.
questions.source: SSC CGL — Ryotwari System with Thomas Munro and Madras is a standard revenue system identification question

questions.text: The Mahalwari System, introduced in the North-Western Provinces, differed from Permanent Settlement and Ryotwari in that it:
options: Permanently fixed revenue so zamindars always knew what they owed
Settled revenue with individual cultivators with no middlemen
Settled revenue with the entire village community (mahal), with the headman responsible for collection
Abolished all revenue demands and replaced them with a direct income tax on agricultural profit
questions.explanation: The Mahalwari System (introduced by Holt Mackenzie in 1822, revised by R. Merttins Bird in 1833) settled revenue with the village community (mahal) as a whole, not with individual zamindars or individual cultivators. The village headman (lambardar or muqaddam) was responsible for collecting from each villager and depositing with the government. Revenue was periodically revised — not permanently fixed like the Permanent Settlement. It was applied in the North-Western Provinces (present-day UP), Punjab, and parts of Central India.
questions.source: SSC CGL — the defining feature of Mahalwari (village community settlement) is a standard distinguish-the-systems question

questions.text: Which of the following correctly identifies the regions where each land revenue system was applied?
options: Permanent Settlement: Madras; Ryotwari: Bengal; Mahalwari: Punjab
Permanent Settlement: Bengal, Bihar, Orissa; Ryotwari: Madras, Bombay; Mahalwari: NW Provinces, Punjab
Permanent Settlement: NW Provinces; Ryotwari: Bengal, Assam; Mahalwari: Madras, Bombay
Permanent Settlement: Bengal, Madras; Ryotwari: Punjab; Mahalwari: Bombay, Orissa
questions.explanation: The three land revenue systems were applied in distinct regions: Permanent Settlement (Zamindari) in Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa (and later parts of northern Madras and Benaras); Ryotwari in Madras Presidency, Bombay Presidency, Assam, and Coorg; Mahalwari in the North-Western Provinces (present-day UP), Punjab, and parts of Madhya Pradesh. Ryotwari covered the largest area (~51% of British India), Mahalwari ~30%, and Permanent Settlement ~19%.
questions.source: SSC CGL — region matching for the three revenue systems is one of the most tested formats; students often swap Bengal (Permanent) with Madras (Ryotwari)

questions.text: The Permanent Settlement permanently fixed revenue — and this turned out to be the British government's biggest financial mistake. Why?
options: Zamindars could not pay the fixed amount and frequently defaulted, leading to revenue losses
As agricultural productivity and land values rose over the following century, the Company received the same fixed amount while zamindars pocketed all the growing surplus
The fixed revenue was set too high, driving zamindars into bankruptcy and causing widespread land abandonment
It prevented the Company from taxing newly cultivated land, so large tracts remained untaxed
questions.explanation: The Company's error was fixing the revenue permanently in 1793 prices. As the 19th century progressed, agricultural productivity improved, land values rose dramatically, and new crops (especially cash crops like jute and indigo) became highly profitable. But the Company was locked into its 1793 revenue — it got the same amount regardless of how productive Bengal became. Zamindars pocketed the entire surplus between what they paid the Company and what they extracted from cultivators. The British themselves later estimated they had permanently surrendered roughly 2/3 of Bengal's potential revenue. This is why the Ryotwari and Mahalwari systems both included periodic revision of assessments.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 'mistake' angle of Permanent Settlement (why it was bad for the Company, not just for cultivators) is a nuanced analytical question in higher-difficulty papers

questions.text: Ryotwari was designed to help cultivators by removing landlord middlemen. In practice, why did it still cause hardship?
options: Revenue was permanently fixed at too high a level, making adjustment impossible in bad years

Revenue rates were often set at 50-55% of gross produce; periodic upward revisions added burden; cultivators unable to pay in bad years borrowed from moneylenders and lost land to moneylenders instead of zamindars
The British used Ryotwari to force cultivators to grow only cash crops, destroying food security
Ryotwari was identical to Permanent Settlement in practice — the government collected through zamindars who were renamed 'ryot agents'
questions.explanation: Despite removing the zamindar middleman, Ryotwari failed cultivators because: (1) Assessment rates were set at 50-55% of gross produce — far too high for sustainable farming; (2) Periodic revisions could spike the demand when the assessment period expired; (3) In years of drought or crop failure, cultivators couldn't pay — they borrowed from moneylenders at crushing interest rates, and if unable to repay, lost their land to the moneylender. So the Ryotwari system replaced the zamindar-as-oppressor with the moneylender-as-oppressor. Either way, the actual cultivator suffered. R.C. Dutt, in his 'Economic History of India' (1902), extensively documented how Ryotwari's high assessments impoverished Madras.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because the 'Ryotwari was meant to help but didn't' analysis is a higher-order thinking question that appears in competitive exams

questions.text: Which revenue system is most closely linked to the Indigo Revolt of 1859-60 in Bengal, and why?
options: Ryotwari — because ryots in Madras refused to grow indigo on the compulsory 3/20 kathas rule
Mahalwari — because village communities collectively organized the indigo boycott
Permanent Settlement — because zamindars (and European planters who leased zamindar land) forced cultivators to grow indigo under the 'tinkathia' system
All three systems equally — indigo cultivation was mandatory across all regions of British India
questions.explanation: The Indigo Revolt (1859-60) in Bengal is a direct consequence of the Permanent Settlement. European indigo planters either became zamindars themselves or leased land from them. Under the 'tinkathia' system, cultivators were forced to grow indigo on 3 out of every 20 kathas (3/20 of land) of their land — the term 'tinkathia' (three kathas) comes from this ratio. Indigo was profitable for planters but barely covered cultivation costs for farmers. When Bengali cultivators collectively refused to grow indigo, it became a major organized peasant revolt — one of the first such movements in India. Dinabandhu Mitra's play *Nil Darpan* (1860) dramatized the planters' brutality and was translated by Reverend James Long.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — anticipated because Indigo Revolt is taught separately but its connection to the Permanent Settlement (land tenure system) is a specific link tested in integrated questions

questions.text: Why did the British introduce the Permanent Settlement despite it locking them into a fixed revenue forever?
options: Lord Cornwallis wanted to reward zamindars who had helped suppress the 1757 Plassey conspirators
The British wanted a stable, predictable revenue base and a loyal landed class without the administrative burden of directly managing millions of cultivators
The Company needed permanent revenue to fund the Napoleonic Wars in Europe
Philip Francis, not Cornwallis, was the real architect and he wanted to create an English-style gentry in India
questions.explanation: The British motivation was pragmatic: (1) Administrative convenience — managing revenue from millions of individual cultivators would require an enormous bureaucracy; dealing with a few thousand zamindars was far simpler; (2) Stable, predictable revenue — the Company needed to plan its finances; a permanent settlement gave certainty; (3) Creating a loyal class — zamindars given hereditary ownership had a stake in maintaining British rule; they became a buffer between the Company and the peasant masses. Philip Francis (member of the Supreme Council) did advocate for the Permanent Settlement earlier, but Cornwallis implemented it. The long-term mistake was not foreseeing agricultural growth.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because students know *what* the Permanent Settlement was, but rarely know *why* the British chose it (the strategic reasoning); appears in analytical questions

questions.text: The Mahalwari System was revised and standardized by which official, and in which year?
options: Thomas Munro, 1820
Philip Francis, 1793
R. Merttins Bird, 1833
Holt Mackenzie, 1793
questions.explanation: The Mahalwari System was first introduced by Holt Mackenzie in 1822 in the North-Western Provinces. However, it was R. Merttins Bird who significantly revised and standardized it in 1833, making it more systematic and applicable across the NW Provinces. Bird's revision is considered the mature form of the Mahalwari system. This two-step history (Mackenzie introduced; Bird revised) is an exam trap — questions sometimes ask for 'who revised' rather than 'who introduced'. The system was later modified further for Punjab after its annexation (1849).
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because Mahalwari is the least-tested of the three systems, and the two-step Mackenzie-Bird history is a detail that appears in specific fact-recall questions

questions.text: How did the land revenue systems connect to the frequency of famines in British India?
options: The revenue systems had no connection to famines — famines were purely caused by monsoon failures

Fixed revenue demands forced farmers to sell food crops even in bad years; commercialization of agriculture (cotton, indigo, opium instead of food) reduced food security; revenue-driven debt led to distress sales — all multiplying the impact of any drought
Famines only occurred in Ryotwari regions because Permanent Settlement zamindars stockpiled food
Revenue demands were automatically waived during famines, so revenue systems had a protective role
questions.explanation: The land revenue systems were structurally connected to famines: (1) To pay cash revenue, cultivators had to grow cash crops (cotton for Bombay mills, indigo for British export, opium for China trade) instead of food — reducing local food production; (2) Even in drought years, revenue demand continued — cultivators who couldn't pay sold food stocks to pay revenue, converting drought into starvation; (3) Revenue-driven debt trapped cultivators in a cycle that reduced their resilience to bad harvests. R.C. Dutt's 'Economic History of India' and Dadabhai Naoroji's 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India' (1901) extensively documented this connection. The Great Bengal Famine (1770) — under Company control, not yet formalized revenue systems — killed an estimated 10 million; later famines under each system followed similar patterns.
questions.source: Not an official PYQ — underrated because the revenue-famine connection is a structural analysis question rarely covered in basic notes but appears in higher-difficulty integrated questions

questions.text: Which of the following is the CORRECT match of land revenue system, region, and the person who introduced it?
options: Permanent Settlement — Madras — Thomas Munro; Ryotwari — Bengal — Cornwallis; Mahalwari — Punjab — Holt Mackenzie
Permanent Settlement — Bengal, Bihar, Orissa — Lord Cornwallis; Ryotwari — Madras, Bombay — Thomas Munro; Mahalwari — NW Provinces, Punjab — Holt Mackenzie
Permanent Settlement — NW Provinces — Warren Hastings; Ryotwari — Bengal — Lord Wellesley; Mahalwari — Madras — Thomas Munro
Permanent Settlement — Bengal — Lord Dalhousie; Ryotwari — NW Provinces — R.M. Bird; Mahalwari — Bombay — Elphinstone
questions.explanation: The three land revenue systems: (1) Permanent Settlement (1793) — Bengal, Bihar, Orissa — Lord Cornwallis (zamindars became permanent landowners; revenue fixed forever). (2) Ryotwari (1820 in Madras) — Madras Presidency, Bombay — Thomas Munro (direct settlement with cultivators; revenue periodically revised). (3) Mahalwari (1822, revised 1833) — NW Provinces, Punjab, parts of MP — Holt Mackenzie / R. Merttins Bird (settlement with village community; periodically revised). This 3x3 matching (system × region × person) is the single most tested format for this topic in SSC CGL.
questions.source: Match the revenue system to region/person — the most frequently tested question format for this topic across SSC CGL Tier I and Tier II papers

questions.text: Under the Permanent Settlement (1793), what happened to a zamindar who failed to pay revenue on the due date?
options: He was given a grace period of one month to arrange payment
His land was auctioned, under the 'Sunrise Rule' — if payment was not deposited before sunrise on the due date, the estate was auctioned
He was imprisoned until his heirs paid the arrears
His revenue was enhanced by 25% as a penalty but his ownership remained intact
questions.explanation: The 'Sunrise Rule' of the Permanent Settlement required zamindars to deposit the fixed annual revenue before sunrise on the due date. If payment was not made before sunrise, the estate was immediately auctioned to the highest bidder. This created constant anxiety — zamindars had to be absolutely punctual regardless of circumstances (crop failures, monsoon failures, family crises). Many zamindars lost their estates under this rule; new bidders (including rich merchants and moneylenders) bought estates and became new zamindars with no prior connection to the land. The Sunrise Rule is a frequently tested specific feature of the Permanent Settlement.
questions.source: The Sunrise Rule and what happens on non-payment under Permanent Settlement is a standard specific-feature question in SSC CGL Modern History

questions.text: Which land revenue system covered the largest area of British India?
options: Permanent Settlement — because it was introduced first (1793) and expanded everywhere
Mahalwari — because the NW Provinces and Punjab were the most populous
Ryotwari — covering approximately 51% of British India's land area
All three systems covered equal areas as British policy was to apply them uniformly
questions.explanation: Ryotwari covered the largest proportion of British India — approximately 51% of the total land area. This is because it applied to the large Madras and Bombay Presidencies, as well as Assam and Coorg. Mahalwari covered approximately 30% (NW Provinces, Punjab, parts of MP), and Permanent Settlement covered approximately 19% (Bengal, Bihar, Orissa). Despite being introduced first and being the most famous of the three systems, Permanent Settlement actually covered the smallest proportion of British India. The Ryotwari area also had the most direct revenue relationship (no landlord intermediary), making it particularly significant for economic history.
questions.source: Coverage area comparison (which system covered the most land) is a frequently tested 'surprise fact' question — most students assume Permanent Settlement covered the most area
